sources.reviewedCode:
# Time:  O(n^2)
# Space: O(n)

# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: A province is just a component; once one city is claimed, finish that whole group before counting again.
class Solution(object):
    def findCircleNum(self, M):
        """
        :type M: List[List[int]]
        :rtype: int
        """

        class UnionFind(object):
            def __init__(self, n):
                # [Reader note] State setup
                # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Reading an adjacency matrix, component counting, and mapping the same connected-region idea to a new representation.
                self.set = range(n)
                self.count = n

            def find_set(self, x):
               if self.set[x] != x:
                   self.set[x] = self.find_set(self.set[x])  # path compression.
               return self.set[x]

            def union_set(self, x, y):
                x_root, y_root = map(self.find_set, (x, y))
                if x_root != y_root:
                    self.set[min(x_root, y_root)] = max(x_root, y_root)
                    self.count -= 1

        circles = UnionFind(len(M))
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        for i in xrange(len(M)):
            for j in xrange(len(M)):
                # [Reader note] Decision point
                # Pay attention here: The challenge is small, but the matrix representation can tempt you into scanning redundantly if you are not careful.
                if M[i][j] and i != j:
                    circles.union_set(i, j)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return circles.count

sources.reviewedCode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: A province is just a component; once one city is claimed, finish that whole group before counting again.
class Solution:
    def findCircleNum(self, isConnected: List[List[int]]) -> int:
        def dfs(i: int):
            vis[i] = True
            # [Reader note] Main pass
            # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
            for j, x in enumerate(isConnected[i]):
                # [Reader note] Decision point
                # Pay attention here: The challenge is small, but the matrix representation can tempt you into scanning redundantly if you are not careful.
                if not vis[j] and x:
                    dfs(j)

        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Reading an adjacency matrix, component counting, and mapping the same connected-region idea to a new representation.
        n = len(isConnected)
        vis = [False] * n
        ans = 0
        for i in range(n):
            if not vis[i]:
                dfs(i)
                ans += 1
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return ans

writeups.title: 547.Friend-Circles
writeups.excerpt: ### 547.Friend-Circles 注意，最后需要重新遍历一遍所有节点，通过FindSet来更新所有的父节点。 ```cpp unordered_set<int>Set; for (int i=0; i<N; i++) { Father[i] = FindSet(i); Set.insert(Father[i]); } return Set.size(); ``` [Leetcode Link](https://leetcode.com/problems/friend-circles)
